pattern: The AND/OR pairing lets you freely redistribute, for each bit position independently, which elements end up holding that bit, so the achievable final arrays are exactly those matching the original per-bit population counts -- and squares are maximized by piling as many high bits as possible onto as few numbers as possible.
hints: Only the count of numbers carrying each bit position matters -- the AND/OR trade lets you move a bit from one element to another at will, so think column by column across the binary representations rather than element by element.

To build the k values you'll square and add, decide bit by bit (from most to least significant) how many of the k slots that bit's count can cover, and always fill in the same k slots first -- concentration beats spreading out because squaring rewards larger single numbers.

Construct k numbers one at a time: for the next number, greedily take one occurrence of every bit position that still has any occurrences left, then move to the next number -- sum their squares modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: small mixed array, choosing fewer than all elements
choosing every element, no rearrangement needed to beat the trivial sum
single element array with k equal to the array length
k equal to the full array length with several duplicate values
a value at the top of the allowed range paired with all-zero-bit partners
k is 1, so every bit should be concentrated into the single chosen number
all elements identical, redistribution changes nothing
powers of two with no overlapping bits, testing that bits still get concentrated correctly

stress.description: large random array with random 30-bit values and a random k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums, k]

stress.description: smaller array with heavily overlapping low bits to stress the concentration logic
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 500))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 15) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums, k]

complexity.time: O(n log(max value) + k log(max value))
